summary_text:
S:
The patient reports a runny nose and congestion, starting around supper time last night. He also reports a sore throat and pulling on his ears. He's been irritable. He's been using saline mist and aveno shampoo on his cradle cap. He's been giving his baby half a teaspoon of sister's medicine. He's been offering his baby agave instead of honey. The patient suspects a viral infection.

O:
The patient's rectal temperature was 100.3°F. He exhibited signs of mild distress, including rubbing at his nose and sweating. Examination revealed mild nasal congestion. Examination of the ears revealed no acute findings. The patient's heart rate was within normal limits.

A:
The doctor suspects a sinus infection. The patient's symptoms are consistent with a viral infection, possibly complicated by a sinus infection.

P:
The doctor will give the patient half a teaspoon of sister's medicine. The doctor will give the patient sister's medicine. The doctor recommends increasing oral hydration and slow positional changes. The doctor recommends follow-up if symptoms worsen or do not improve.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Cough, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Randy Gutierrez is a 9-month-old male who is being evaluated today for a cough, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea. He is accompanied by his mother.

Symptoms began yesterday around dinnertime with clear nasal drainage, pulling on his ears bilaterally, and rubbing his nose frequently. He does not have a barky cough or a sore throat. His rectal temperature was 100.3 degrees. The patient did consume a 7 ounce bottle this morning, but mostly denied the next offered bottle and declined cereal. He is irritable and having trouble falling asleep, described “like he is fighting it”.

The patient has been exposed to an older sister who is sick and a cousin who has a sinus infection.

Treatment has included saline nasal spray, Tylenol, baby Vick's, and a humidifier. When the patient aroused this morning, he was warm to the touch. When his sleeper was removed, he was quite sweaty despite the air conditioner being on.

The patient's dandruff is also worsening. The only tried treatment is Aveeno shampoo.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Older sister.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Tylenol PRN.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Temperature 100.3 degrees.

Ears, Eyes, Nose, Throat
Nasal congestion, clear mucus, rhinorrhea. Bilateral pulling of ears, no sore throat or hoarseness.

Respiratory
Positive for cough.

ASSESSMENT

• Viral infection
• Dandruff

PLAN

Viral infection
The patient presents with a viral infection and does not need antibiotics currently. I advised continuation of fluids, rest. Additionally, I suggested trying Zarbee’s for children under 1 years old. If a croup cough develops, the patient will receive a 0.5 teaspoon.

I also provided counseling to the mother to avoid giving honey to the patient for his first year of life due to botulism spores.

Dandruff
The patient is experiencing worsening symptoms of dandruff. I recommended Head and Shoulders shampoo, Selsun Blue, or Nizoral A-D 2 times per week, combined with his usual shampoo. I counseled the mother that she should be careful not to get the shampoo in the patient eyes and to not use the extra strength shampoos. Additionally, he may also use baby oil and a soft brush on the area.

INSTRUCTIONS

Return in one week for a re-check.

sections.section_value: The doctor will give the patient half a teaspoon of sister's medicine. The doctor will give the patient sister's medicine. The doctor recommends increasing oral hydration and slow positional changes. The doctor recommends follow-up if symptoms worsen or do not improve.

section_miscategorizations.text: The doctor will give the patient half a teaspoon of sister's medicine.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is framed as a treatment plan, but it directly contradicts the subjective history that the caregiver has already been inappropriately giving a sibling's medicine. As written, it misleads the reader into thinking this is an intentional, appropriate medical order rather than unsafe prior behavior. It should be rephrased or clarified as counseling to stop this practice, not as an active plan to administer it.

section_miscategorizations.text: The doctor will give the patient sister's medicine.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Same issue as above: although structurally a plan item, it is clinically misleading because it presents giving another child's medicine as an intended medical order rather than addressing it as unsafe prior use. It should be removed from the plan or reframed as counseling against this practice.
